headline: Core contracts verified on Etherscan with UUPS proxy + implementation both verified; multiple recognized-firm audits exist, though some drift from latest deployment warrants noting

findings.text: SSVNetwork proxy (0xDD9BC35aE942eF0cFa76930954a156B3fF30a4E6) and SSVNetworkViews proxy (0xafE92929605C7e0e0fCB93b8e1c4BFd5F6C8f4e6) are verified on Etherscan under the UUPS upgradeable proxy pattern. Both current implementation contracts are also independently verified: SSVNetwork implementation at 0x5F92F0CF48b5aC7eb0e3fd0df41e83Df3d86A41F (Contract Source Code Verified) and SSVNetworkViews implementation at 0xA55E2C308A23C4A08DB11Ef54A95b67B19A97C5B. The proxy pattern ('Similar Match' ERC1967/UUPS) is expected for this architecture and does not constitute a verification gap.

The GitHub repository https://github.com/ssvlabs/ssv-network contains the Solidity source for SSVNetwork.sol, SSVNetworkViews.sol, and related libraries. The explorer-visible source corresponds structurally and in content to the public repo. A precise commit SHA pinning to the deployed bytecode was not confirmed via local compilation this run; this is recorded in unknowns[].

Multiple audit reports are publicly linked in the SSV docs and GitHub security folder: (1) Quantstamp audit of v1.0 contracts (2023-01, https://github.com/ssvlabs/ssv-network/blob/main/docs/audits/Quantstamp-SSV-Network-Audit-Report.pdf); (2) Certora formal verification (2023, https://github.com/ssvlabs/ssv-network/blob/main/docs/audits/Certora-SSV-Network-Formal-Verification.pdf); (3) Quantstamp v1.1 update audit (2023-07). The most recent publicly listed audit covers the v1.1 contracts. Given the protocol has since shipped v1.2+ updates and the snapshot date is April 2026, there is potential drift of >6 months between latest audited commit and current deployment.

Auditors include Quantstamp and Certora, both broadly recognized firms in the Solidity/EVM security ecosystem. Quantstamp is a recognized audit firm; Certora is recognized for formal verification. This satisfies the recognized-firm requirement.

The deployed contracts on mainnet reflect SSV Network v1.2.x or later (as of early 2026), while the most recent publicly referenced audit covers v1.1 (July 2023). This represents a post-audit drift period exceeding 6 months without a publicly confirmed follow-up audit for the latest deployed version. This is a downgrade signal toward orange.

Both proxy contracts (SSVNetwork and SSVNetworkViews) have independently verified implementation contracts on Etherscan. The proxy is verified and its current implementation is also verified — no unverified-implementation gap exists.

steelman.red: No audit links are recorded in the pinned protocol metadata, and if the most recent publicly accessible audit predates the current deployment by over 18 months with non-trivial contract changes (new module system, updated fee logic), the protocol is effectively unaudited at its current deployed state.
steelman.orange: While bytecode is verified and recognized firms conducted audits, the most recent audit covers v1.1 (mid-2023) while the deployed code is v1.2+ (2024-2026), creating >6 months of post-audit drift on materially updated contracts without a confirmed follow-up audit publicly linked.
steelman.green: Both proxy and implementation contracts are fully verified on Etherscan, source code corresponds to a public GitHub repo, and multiple recognized firms (Quantstamp, Certora) have audited the core contracts; the protocol's architecture and security posture are independently verifiable by any outsider.

rationale.verdict: Choosing orange because while V1 and V6 are satisfied (proxy + implementation both verified on Etherscan) and V4 is satisfied (Quantstamp and Certora are recognized firms), V5 reveals material post-audit drift: the most recent confirmed audit covers v1.1 (July 2023) while the currently deployed contracts reflect v1.2+ changes shipped in 2024, exceeding the 6-month threshold without a publicly confirmed re-audit. The pinned protocol.audit_links is empty, which independently flags the audit coverage as unconfirmed for current deployment. This combination — verified bytecode, recognized past auditors, but stale audit coverage relative to current deployment — fits the orange grade definition ('audit scope is stale relative to deployment').

evidence.shows: SSVNetwork UUPS proxy contract on Ethereum mainnet — Contract Source Code Verified
SSVNetworkViews UUPS proxy contract on Ethereum mainnet — Contract Source Code Verified
Official SSV Network deployed contract addresses for Ethereum mainnet including SSVNetwork proxy and SSVNetworkViews proxy
Primary SSV Network Solidity source repository (ssvlabs org), containing SSVNetwork.sol, SSVNetworkViews.sol, and audit reports in docs/audits/
Quantstamp audit report for SSV Network v1.0 contracts (January 2023)
Audit reports directory in SSV Network GitHub repo listing all published security reviews including Quantstamp v1.0, Certora formal verification, and Quantstamp v1.1

unknowns: V2: No local bytecode compilation or diff was performed against a pinned deploy commit SHA — structural/content correspondence to the public repo was assessed visually, not via compile-match.

V3: No audit URL was provided in protocol.audit_links; audit reports were located via GitHub repo search. A post-v1.1 re-audit (covering v1.2+ changes shipped 2024-2026) could not be confirmed from available sources.

V5: The exact commit SHA of the currently deployed implementation contracts was not pinned and compared against the latest audited commit — post-audit drift is inferred from version numbering and release history rather than confirmed line-by-line diff.

admin_addresses.role: SSV DAO multisig (proxy admin / upgrade authority)

protocol_metadata.about: SSV Network is a decentralized Ethereum validator infrastructure protocol that splits a validator's private key into KeyShares distributed among a set of independent node operators using Distributed Validator Technology (DVT), enabling fault-tolerant and trust-minimized staking. Stakers register validators by selecting operators and paying fees in SSV tokens; operators run the validator duties across multiple machines so no single party holds the full key. The protocol is permissionless for both operators and stakers, and is governed by an SSV DAO using the SSV token.
